Certainly! Here's a letter from a grizzled Victorian-era lighthouse keeper to his estranged daughter, written as the storm approaches and he's uncertain of his fate:

---

**To My Dearest Elise,**

If you're reading this, then I've either been lost to the sea or the storm has taken me. Either way, I hope these words reach you, and that you're safe, and that you've found peace in your own way.

Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I've spent them in silence, watching the waves and the endless sky. I've been a lighthouse keeper for most of my life, and I've seen many storms. But this one feels different. It carries a weight that I can't explain, and I fear it may take more than just the light from the tower.

I know you've been angry with me, Elise. I know you've blamed me for leaving you, for not being there when you needed me. And I can't blame you for that. I was a fool, and I made choices that hurt the ones I loved most. I thought I was protecting you, that I was doing the right thing by staying here, by keeping the light burning for those who might need it. But I was wrong. I should have been there for you, not just in words, but in heart.

I've spent so many nights thinking about you, wondering if you've found someone who will love you

Ah, my dear Eliza,

It has been five long years since I last set pen to paper, and five even longer since I last spoke to you in anything but the harshest of tones. The sea has been unkind to me, and the wind has carried my words away like the foam upon the shore. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself compelled to write, for I fear the sea may take me before I can say what I must.

You were once so full of life, my darling, with eyes like the dawn and a heart that beat with the rhythm of the tides. I remember the day you left, the way your hands trembled as you clutched the doorframe, and the way your voice cracked with emotion as you whispered, "I must go, Father." I did not understand then, and I still do not, but I know that you believed it was the right thing. And I, in my stubborn pride, allowed it.

The lighthouse stands strong, though the wind howls like a wounded beast and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury I have never known. The beacon burns bright, as it always has, a guiding light for those who sail the perilous waters. It is the only thing I have left to keep me company, save for the whispers of the sea and the memories of you.

I have not been a good father, Eliza. I have been a man consumed

*The candle flickers, casting long shadows across the stone walls of the lighthouse. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the windows. The lighthouse keeper, a man of rugged features and weathered hands, sits at his desk, a quill in hand, his voice rough with age and years of silence.*

**Lighthouse Keeper:**  
*My dear Eleanor,*

*The sea has been restless these past few nights, and I’ve felt it in my bones. The waves have grown more furious, as though they carry the weight of all the years I’ve kept silent. I’ve kept this lighthouse lit for thirty years, through tempests and calm, through the cold and the dark. But tonight... tonight is different.*

*I don’t know if I’ll make it through the storm. The wind is not like the others. It howls with a voice I’ve heard before, though I’ve tried to forget. I’ve tried to forget you. I’ve tried to forget the day you left, the day you told me you’d never come back. I’ve tried to forget the silence that followed, the silence that grew like a rot in my heart.*

*But the storm is coming, and I’m not sure I’ll be here to see the dawn. And if I am, I’ve decided I’ll not be the man who let silence swallow me whole.*

*I’ve written this letter, not to beg for your forgiveness, for I know

**To My Dearest and Only Daughter,**

**In the Month of September, in the Year of Our Lord 1843**

**From the Lighthouse of St. Agnes, upon the Coast of the Northern Sea**

I write to thee, my child, with trembling hand and a heart heavy with sorrow, for I have not heard thy voice in five long years, and the weight of time doth press upon me like the storm that now gathers upon the horizon. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves doth rise as if to swallow the very earth, and I fear that I may not live to see another dawn. Yet, in this hour of peril, I find myself compelled to write, for I have a final word to speak to thee, and a final plea to make.

Thou art not a stranger to the trials of life, my dear, for thou hast known sorrow and strife, and yet I have heard naught of thee since the day thou didst leave our home. I have not seen thy face, nor heard thy voice, nor felt thy touch, and it is this absence that doth torment me more than the tempest that now doth rage. I have kept the lamp alight, as I have always done, for it is my duty to the Crown and to the mariners who sail these perilous waters, yet I have no light to guide me in the darkness of my soul.

I have heard whispers

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**If you are reading this, then I am either dead or you have returned to this desolate shore. Either way, I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five long years, and the silence has grown heavier with each passing day.**

The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have stood on this cliff for many years, guiding ships through the dark and the storm, but tonight... tonight feels different. The sky is blacker than the night before the world was made, and the waves crash with a fury that seems almost... *intentional*. I have not seen such a storm in my time, and I fear it may be the end of all things.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent word of my whereabouts. I know you do not care for a man like me—*a lighthouse keeper, a fool who chose to live in the shadows of the sea rather than the light of the world*. But I have not forgotten you, Eliza. Not in the five years since you left, nor in the many nights I’ve stood alone, the lantern flickering like a dying heart.

You were a bright child, full of fire and spirit. I once thought you would grow into a woman of strength and grace. But you chose your own path, and I chose mine. I thought that by

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in the best of health and spirits, though I fear that, in these trying times, the winds of fate may have carried your heart far from this old lighthouse and the shore where I have stood for so many years. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I must confess that I have often found myself gazing out to sea, hoping to see the familiar silhouette of your boat or the gentle curve of your hat as you walked along the promenade. Yet, I have seen neither.

Tonight, as the sun dips below the horizon and the sky darkens with a storm that promises to be fierce, I find myself with a heart heavy with both sorrow and longing. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves, once gentle, now crash against the rocks with a fury that stirs memories of our past. I have always believed that the sea reveals the truest of souls, and I wonder if it has shown you the truth of your own heart, or if it has merely carried you away from the one who once held you close.

I have often thought of the days when we would sit together on the beach, the stars above us as we shared tales of our lives. You were always so full of dreams, and I, though content in my solitude, would often find myself longing to see you again. I have written many letters, each one filled with words that I have never

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the sea roars with a fury that seems almost human. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, but if I do, I must say these words—words I have long kept buried beneath the weight of shame, regret, and the unspoken.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken your name in all that time. Yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the lighthouse stands alone against the darkness, I find myself longing for your voice. Not as a child, but as a daughter—strong, independent, and unyielding, just as I once hoped you would be.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. I know you have built a life far from this desolate shore, far from the cold stone walls of this tower where I have spent my days watching the sea. You have your own world, your own purpose, and I have no right to intrude. But I must tell you this: I have not forgotten you. I have not stopped loving you.

There was a time when I believed that my silence would be a shield. That by not reaching out, I would protect you from the pain of my failures. But I was wrong. Silence does not protect—it consumes. I have spent these years haunted by the thought of what

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast at the door, the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. Yet, I must write, for if I do not, I fear the silence between us may never be broken.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years of silence have followed. I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt your presence in the cold, salt-laden air of this lighthouse. You were my light, Eleanor. My only light. And now, I fear that light has long since faded from your heart.

I have kept this lighthouse for more than thirty years, watching the tides, the storms, the endless cycles of the sea. It has been my sanctuary, my burden, my purpose. But now, as the wind howls and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury I have not known in years, I feel the weight of this place more than ever. I feel the weight of my own failing body, and the weight of the silence that has settled between us.

You were once the reason I rose before dawn, to tend the lamp, to keep the light burning for those who sail these dark waters. You were the reason I smiled, even on the loneliest nights. And yet, when you left, I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I. I write with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret, for I have not spoken to you in five long years. Time has worn away the edges of our once-pleasant conversations, and I fear it has also eroded the love that once bound us together.

The wind howls outside like the very devil himself, and I sit here in the lighthouse, the great lamp flickering uncertainly in the storm's approach. I have kept this place for over twenty years, and though I have seen many storms, none have ever made me feel so small, so fragile. I do not know if I will survive the night, and if I do not, I pray this letter will reach you.

You were but a child when you left, Eleanor. I remember the day you stood at the door, your eyes filled with tears and your heart full of sorrow. You said you needed to find your own way, and I, in my pride and stubbornness, let you go. I did not realize then that you would not return. I did not realize that the silence between us would grow louder with each passing year.

I have tried, my dear, I have tried to forgive you. I have tried to understand. But the pain of your absence lingers like the smoke from the lamp that never quite fades. I have spent these years in quiet solitude, keeping the light burning for those who

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you with a quill that trembles, not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since our last exchange, and though the sea has carried many a letter, none have come from you. I have read the tide charts, studied the stars, and yet I cannot find the path to your heart.

The night is upon us, my child, and the heavens grow dark with the promise of a storm unlike any we have known. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves, once so gentle, now rage with a fury that shakes the very stones of this cliff. I have spent my life watching the horizon, guiding ships through the perilous waters, but tonight, I fear I may not see the dawn.

I have not written to you because I feared what I might say. You were but a girl when you left, and I could not bear the thought of you standing before me, cold and unyielding. I told myself that time would mend what was broken, that the distance would fade. But time has only deepened the chasm between us.

You were always my light, Eleanor. I remember the day you came to me as a child, no older than ten, and asked if the sea had a soul. I told you it did, and that it watched over all who called it home. You believed me, and in that belief, I found my purpose

**April 3rd, 1897**  
**Lighthouse, Skerryvore, Isle of Cumbrae**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with the ink of a man who has long since lost the use of his hands, and the voice of one who has forgotten how to speak without trembling. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea, that ancient and unrelenting beast, roars in protest against the heavens. The lighthouse stands, as it always has, a sentinel of stone and flame, but I fear it may not stand much longer.  

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left me, and five years since I ceased to be the man you once knew. You were a child then, or so you called me. A child with a heart like a storm and a tongue like a scythe. I remember the last time we spoke, the way your eyes burned with something I could not name, and the way your voice cut through the silence like a blade. You said I was cruel. That I had no soul. That I had forgotten how to love. And I let you say it. I let you go.  

But I have not forgotten. Not entirely.  

The storm is coming, Eleanor. It is not the kind that merely shakes the windows or drenches the rocks. It is the kind that rages with the fury of the damned, and I fear it may take

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though I have not forgotten you, I fear you have long since forgotten me. The sea, as ever, is a cruel and fickle mistress, and tonight, it is rising with a fury that has not been seen in generations. The wind howls like the wails of lost souls, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a violence that shakes the very bones of this old lighthouse.

I have spent my life standing at this post, guiding ships through the darkest nights and the most treacherous waters. But tonight, I find myself questioning whether I shall be the one to guide them home. The storm is unlike any I have known, and the light that has always been my steadfast companion seems dimmer, as if the very heavens are weeping with me.

I have not written to you in so long, and yet I have thought of you often. I have wondered what path you have taken, what world you have built beyond the shores of our childhood. I have tried to imagine you as a woman, not the child who once clung to my coat and whispered prayers as I climbed the tower. I have tried to forgive the last words we shared, the way your voice broke as you turned away, and I have tried to understand the pain that must have driven you to leave.

**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**If you're reading this, then I have not made it through the night. I hope you find this letter in time, and that it reaches you before the storm devours everything.**

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke. Five years of silence, of regret, and of a heart that aches with longing. I’ve tried to forget you, to bury the memories of your last words, the way your eyes turned cold and distant when you left. But the sea has a way of whispering your name in the wind, and the lighthouse has become my prison, my confession, and my only companion.

You were once my greatest pride. I taught you the ways of the sea, the secrets of the light, the patience required to keep the beacon burning through the darkest nights. I told you stories of storms that tested the strength of men, of ships that vanished without a trace, and of the power that lies in the light. I believed I was preparing you for a life of purpose, of duty, of meaning. But I was wrong.

You left. Not because you no longer wanted to be with me, but because you believed I had failed you. That I had chosen the lighthouse over you. That I had let the sea take the place of a father. I know now that I was blind, that

My dearest Eleanor,

I write these words with trembling hands, the wind howling like the very devil outside these walls. The sea roars in fury, and the sky is black as ink, save for the occasional flash of lightning that strikes the horizon like a warning. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, and if I do, I hope you will read this. I hope you will forgive me.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have spent them in silence, a prisoner to my own guilt and regret. You were but a child when you left, and I, in my foolish pride, did not understand the pain you must have felt. I told you I would always be there, that I would never let the world take you from me. But I was wrong. I was blind to the storm that brewed in your heart, and I did not see the storm that brewed in mine.

I have kept this lighthouse for many years, watching over the sea with the same devotion I once gave you. Each night, I would light the beacon, and in its glow, I would imagine your face, your laughter, your voice. I would speak to you as if you were there, and in those moments, I felt whole again. But now, as the storm approaches, I fear I may be the last to see the light.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. You brought me joy when I had none,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

If you're reading this, then I've not made it through the night. Or perhaps you've found it in the wreckage of the lighthouse, along with the remnants of a life I failed to hold together.

Five years. Five long, bitter years. I've watched the moon rise and set, the tides come and go, and still you never came back. I told myself it was for the best—your own life, your own path. But I've been a fool, and a worse father. I should have reached for you when you left, not let the sea take me instead.

The storm is coming. I've seen the sky turn black with fury, heard the waves grow louder than the thunder. The light's been dimming, and I fear it's not the wind that will take me, but the silence. The silence of a father who never spoke, and a daughter who never listened.

I've always been a man of duty, Eleanor. I kept the light burning for the sailors, for the ships, for the world that passed me by

**My Dearest Eliza,**

**Five years have passed since our last exchange, and in that time, I have been a solitary figure upon this bleak and windswept isle, my days spent in the ceaseless vigil of the lighthouse, and my nights in the company of the sea’s eternal song.** I have seen the moon wax and wane, the stars blink their silent approval, and the gulls cry their ancient lamentations. Yet none of these have brought me the solace of thy presence, nor the comfort of thy voice.

**I have heard the tale of thy marriage, though I have not the heart to inquire into the details, for I fear the truth may be too bitter to bear.** I have heard, too, of thy new home in the city, where the streets are paved with stone and the air is thick with the scent of ambition. **To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the devil himself outside these stone walls. The sea roars in fury, as though it seeks to swallow the very heart of this island. Five years have passed since your last letter, and in that time, I have heard nothing from you, not a single word. I have waited, as one waits for the dawn, for you to return, to come back to this place, to this home. But you have not come. You have not written. And I have not dared to write, for fear that the words would be lost in the sea, like so many others.

I have always been the one to keep the flame burning, to stand alone in the cold and the dark, to guide the ships that pass through these treacherous waters. I have never been the one to turn away. **Letter from a Victorian-era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

**[Date: October 23, 1892]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. Tonight, the storm is upon us, and I fear it may be the end of me. But I must write, for if I do not, I may never have the chance to tell you what I have long kept locked away in my soul.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke. Five years of silence, of bitter words, of a rift that grew deeper with each passing day. I have not seen you, nor have I heard from you. You left, Eleanor, and I did not stop you. I thought I had done the right thing. I thought I was protecting you from the life I live, the life I have chosen. But now, as the storm rages and I stand alone in this lighthouse, I wonder if I was wrong.

You were only a child when you left. A child with dreams of the world beyond this stone fortress, beyond the sea, beyond the life I had built for myself. I tried to understand, to reach out, but I was too proud, too stubborn. I let my anger and fear cloud my judgment. I let the silence grow.

My dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, for I know not whether I shall live to see the dawn. The sea roars like a beast in pain, and the wind howls as if the very heavens weep. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse.

Five years have passed since I last saw you, and in that time, I have come to know a truth I cannot unlearn: that distance breeds silence, and silence breeds sorrow. I have not spoken to you, not even in the language of the waves, and I wonder if I have ever truly known you. I have watched the world shift around me, and yet I have not moved, not even to greet the morning sun.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man who thought himself strong. I told you I would always be there, that the lighthouse would never fall, and that I would never let you go. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was wrong in so many ways.

I have kept the light burning, not for the ships, but for you. I have lit the lantern each night, not because it is my duty, but because I hoped, somehow, that the light would reach you. That you would see it, and know that I was still here, still watching, still waiting.

But you have not come back. And I have not called you. I have

*The ink is smudged from trembling fingers. The wind howls outside, rattling the shutters of the lighthouse. I grip the letter with a calloused hand, the paper curling at the edges like the old shipwrecks I've seen in the sea. The lamp is flickering, the oil low, and the storm is coming. I must write. I must say something. Before it’s too late.*

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the sea howling in my ears and the wind tearing at the shutters like a beast in pain. You were never meant to hear this, but I’ve spent five years pacing these stone floors, counting the hours, the tides, the dead men who’ve come to the rocks and never returned. I’ve been alone, Eliza. Truly alone. And now, with the storm upon us, I feel the weight of the silence more than ever.

You were a fine girl, I’ll admit that. Bright, sharp, and too proud for her own good. You left this place like a ship cutting through the waves, never looking back. I didn’t stop you. I didn’t want to. I thought—*I thought*—that you’d come back when you were ready. That you’d see what it was I was doing here. That you’d understand why I chose this life over the one you wanted.

But five years have passed, and I’ve been alone

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**To Whom It May Concern,**

I trust this missive finds thee in good health and high spirits, though I must confess that I write with a heavy heart, for the weight of years has pressed upon me, and the burden of silence has grown heavier with each passing day.

It hath been five long years since our last discourse, and I must say, I have often found myself gazing upon the horizon, hoping to catch sight of thy familiar form or at least the faintest echo of thy voice. Yet, alas, the sea hath been restless, and the winds have howled like the very demons that once plagued our forebears. I have not heard from thee, nor do I know if thou art still in thy father’s house, or if thou hast taken thy leave of this world altogether, as I have often feared.

I write to thee now not in the spirit of reproach, but with a heavy heart and a soul that hath been sorely troubled. The storm that is coming tonight is of a most terrible nature, and I fear that the wrath of the heavens may yet claim me before the dawn. I have heard the winds howling like the cries of the damned, and the sea hath risen to a height that I have not seen in many a year. The lighthouse, which hath stood firm through countless tempests, now trembles upon its base, and I fear that even it may not withstand the fury

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. The wind howls like a banshee in the night, and the sea, that old and unyielding beast, roars with a fury that makes me fear the very stones upon which I stand. Tonight, I do not know if I shall see the dawn. And if I do, I do not know if I shall see you again.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have not been able to bring myself to write, nor to call, nor to reach out. You were the light in my life, my Clara, and I let it go out. I let it go out because I feared the pain of your answer, the sorrow of your silence, the burden of my own guilt. I was a fool, and I have paid the price in silence, in sorrow, and in the slow, creeping decay of a heart that no longer beats for the world.

You were young, Clara, and I was old. I did not understand the fire that burned within you, the yearning for a life beyond the stones and the salt. I did not understand the choice you made, and I did not understand the pain you bore. I thought I could protect you, but in the end, I only brought you sorrow. I am the reason you left. **Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I must confess I write with a heavy heart, for I have not seen you in five long years, and the weight of that silence grows heavier with each passing day. I trust you are well, and that you have found comfort in the arms of your husband, Thomas, and your little ones. I often find myself recalling the days when you were a child, running about the lighthouse with your brother, and I cannot help but smile at the memory.

Tonight, however, brings with it a storm that threatens to swallow the sea whole. The skies have darkened with a ferocity I have not seen in many years, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have been preparing for this night, as I always do, with the steadfast resolve of a man who knows the sea’s temper. Yet, as I stand upon the cliff, I cannot shake the feeling that this storm may be more than I can weather.

I have always believed in the power of the light, not just as a beacon for the ships that pass, but as a symbol of hope and guidance in the darkest of times. It is this very light that has kept me steadfast through the years, even when the world outside seemed to grow colder and more distant. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the glass of our humble lighthouse. The sea, once a faithful companion, now roars with fury, as though it too mourns the silence between us. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, as I stand alone in this tower, I feel your absence more keenly than ever.

I know you believe I have abandoned you. That I have turned my back on our family, on our home, on the love we once shared. But I ask you, my dear Eleanor, to listen to this letter, if only for a moment, and understand that I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the way your laughter once filled our home, or the way your eyes would light up when you spoke of your dreams. I have not forgotten the pain of your departure, nor the guilt that has clung to me like a shadow for all these years.

I have spent these years tending to this lighthouse, not just as a duty, but as a way to keep a part of you close. The light I keep burning is not merely for the ships that pass by—it is a beacon for the love I have never been able to express. Every beam that pierces the darkness is a prayer for your return, a hope that one day, we may find our way back to each other.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm that gathers beyond the cliffs. The sea howls like a wounded beast, and the wind tears at the sails of my soul. I do not know if I shall see the dawn, but I must speak, for the words may never be said again.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have felt like a lifetime. I have watched the lighthouse burn with the light of your absence, and each night I have stood at the window, hoping to see your silhouette against the moon. But the sea has swallowed your footsteps, and the silence of the coast has grown louder than your voice.

I do not know why you left. I do not know if it was the sea that called you, or the world beyond the rocks and the fog. But I have always believed that you were not cruel, nor was your heart hardened. You were merely lost, as we all are in some way. I have tried to forgive you, but forgiveness is not a thing that comes easily to a man who has spent his life watching the horizon, waiting for something that never comes.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 3rd, 1887**  
**The Lighthouse, Blackmoor Point**

I sit by the window, the sea howling like a wounded beast, and I feel the weight of the night pressing down upon me. The wind has been rising for hours, and the waves have grown fierce, foaming against the rocks with a fury that seems almost unnatural. I have tended the light for over thirty years, and I have seen many storms, but this... this feels different. I fear it may not end well.

And yet, I write to you. Not because I think I will survive this night, but because I have spent five long years thinking about you, and I have come to believe that I must try once more.

You were never the daughter I wanted. You were the daughter I feared. I watched you grow, and I saw the light in your eyes dim, the fire in your heart extinguished. You left without a word, and I have not heard from you since. I have tried to forget, but the nights are long, and the silence of your absence is louder than any storm.

I have no right to ask for your return, not after the way I treated you. I was blind, and I was angry, and I let my pride blind me to the pain I caused. I have spent years regretting that. I have spent years wondering if I would ever be worthy of your

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, not with the ease of a man who has long since reconciled with the world, but with the weight of a soul burdened by silence, by time, by the storm that now howls at the horizon. It is not the tempest that troubles me most, though I fear it may claim me before the dawn. No, it is the thought of you — my daughter, my only kin — that haunts me in the stillness between the thunderclaps.

Five years have passed since our last exchange, and yet it feels like an eternity. I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt the warmth of your presence in this cold, wind-swept tower. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried. I have written, I have sent letters, I have waited for your reply — and always, they have returned unanswered. I have come to believe that you no longer wish to hear from me, or perhaps that you do not wish to be found.

But now, as the sky darkens and the sea rises in fury, I find myself questioning whether it is I who has been lost to the world, or whether it is you.

You were once a child of light, Eleanor — bright, curious, full of life. I watched you grow from a little girl with a penchant for collecting seashells to a young woman with a mind as sharp as the blade of a sailor’s

**April 12th, 1887**  
**Lighthouse, Cape Wrath, Scotland**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old stone. The sea is restless tonight, and the storm that brews on the horizon promises to be the worst this coast has seen in a decade. I fear I may not see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and still I cannot bring myself to forget the sight of your back as you walked out of the door, your cloak trailing behind you like a ghost. You never looked back. I daresay you never wanted to. You were always so *certain* of your path, so convinced that the world would be better without me. You were wrong, Eleanor. I have never been more alone.

The lighthouse has been my only companion these many years. The light burns, though no one is here to see it. The foghorn groans like a wounded beast, and the sea, that relentless, unfeeling thing, keeps its secrets. I have grown used to the silence, but tonight—tonight, the silence is broken by the screams of the wind, and the weight of my own guilt.

I have always been a man of duty, Eleanor. I have kept this light burning for thirty years, through wars, through famine, through the slow decay of the world around me. I have never once

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the sea at dusk. Five years have passed since your last visit, and though I have kept the lighthouse as I always have—its light guiding the lost and the weary—my soul has grown dimmer with each passing day.

You were a child when you left, and I did not understand then the weight of your words. You called me cruel, unfeeling, and unloving. I did not argue, for I feared that to do so would only confirm your suspicions. You were right, in many ways. I have been a man of duty, of stone and salt, and I have forgotten how to be a father.

But now, as the storm brews on the horizon, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. The winds have been growing fierce, the sea restless, and I have heard the old sailors speak of a tempest unlike any in living memory. I do not know if I shall live through it, and if I do, I fear I may not be the same man who once stood at this very post.
